title: Slack / Discord — System Design
description: Design a team-chat platform organized around workspaces and channels: many members share channels, messages fan out to everyone in the channel in real time, history is searchable, and access is permission-controlled. The themes are channel fanout (vs 1:1 chat), the workspace/membership/permission model, searchable history, and presence.

nodes.label: Client
nodes.description: Connects to workspaces/channels; sends and receives messages in real time.

nodes.label: Connection Gateway
nodes.description: Routes WebSocket connections to gateway nodes.

nodes.label: WebSocket Gateway
nodes.description: Holds clients' live connections; relays frames.

nodes.label: Channel Service
nodes.description: Validates, persists, and fans out channel messages.

nodes.label: Message Store
nodes.description: Durable messages, sharded by channel; ordered per channel.

nodes.label: Subscription Registry
nodes.description: Which members are subscribed/connected to each channel (routing).

nodes.label: Fanout Service
nodes.description: Delivers a channel message to all subscribed members' gateways.

nodes.label: Search Index
nodes.description: Full-text index over messages, scoped by permissions.

nodes.label: Search Indexer
nodes.description: Indexes messages for search as they're posted.

functional: Organize into workspaces and channels (public/private); join/leave.
Post a message to a channel; all members see it in real time.
Read channel history and unread state per user.
Search messages (scoped to what the user can access).
nonFunctional: Real-time delivery to all channel members (sub-second).
Reliable + ordered messages within a channel; never lost.
Permission-correct: users only see channels/messages they're allowed to.
Scale to large workspaces and large channels (thousands of members).
Searchable history with low-latency, permission-filtered results.

capacity.label: Connections
capacity.note: Persistent sockets per device, like any chat.

capacity.label: History
capacity.note: Unlike ephemeral chat, search is first-class.

capacity.label: Fanout
capacity.note: N can be large per channel.

api.description: Open a persistent connection; subscribe to the user's channels.
Post a message to a channel; persisted, then fanned out to members.
Search messages, filtered to the requester's accessible channels.

dataModel.name: messages
dataModel.note: Durable channel messages, sharded by channel; ordered per channel.

dataModel.name: channel_membership
dataModel.note: Who is in a channel + their unread cursor + permissions.

dataModel.name: subscriptions (live)
dataModel.note: Routing: which connected gateways have members of a channel.

patterns.name: Channel-centric fanout
patterns.what: Deliver a posted message to all subscribed members of the channel in real time.

patterns.name: Subscription registry
patterns.what: Track which channels each connected client subscribes to so fanout knows where to push.
patterns.whenToUse: Routing channel messages across a sharded gateway fleet.

patterns.name: Per-channel ordering + history
patterns.what: Assign a per-channel sequence; persist durably so history is ordered and replayable.

patterns.name: Permission model (workspace/channel ACL)
patterns.what: Workspaces contain channels; membership + roles gate read/post/search.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-tenant team chat with private channels.

patterns.name: Permission-scoped search
patterns.what: Index messages but filter search results to channels the requester can access.
patterns.whenToUse: Searchable history that must respect access control.

patterns.name: Ephemeral presence
patterns.what: Best-effort online/typing state, separate from the durable message path.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One Channel, Poll for Messages, No Permissions
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. There's effectively one shared channel: a client POSTs a message, the channel service writes it to a single messages table, and every client polls (GET /messages?since=...) to pull new rows. There's no workspace, no membership, no permissions — everyone sees everything.

This is trivial to build and immediately shows the gaps. Polling is high-latency and floods the store with empty reads (the same problem as any chat). Worse for team chat specifically: with no membership or permission model there are no private channels, no way to scope who receives or can search a message, and no notion of which members a message should fan out to. One flat channel doesn't scale to many channels with thousands of members each.

So the baseline motivates the two things that define team chat over 1:1 chat: a workspace/channel/permission model that says who belongs where, and real-time fanout that pushes a posted message to exactly that channel's members.
steps.decisionPrompt: If there's one flat channel everyone polls, what breaks when you add thousands of members, private channels, and permission-scoped search?
whyNow: Establishing the flat-channel polling baseline first makes it obvious why team chat needs both a membership/permission backbone and real-time channel fanout — neither exists yet.

recap.after: One shared channel; messages stored in one table; clients poll for new rows.
recap.newRisk: Polling is high-latency and wasteful, and with no membership/permission model there are no private channels, no scoped delivery, and no scoped search.

traps.trap: Treating team chat as one big chat room with a single message table.
traps.why: Without workspaces, channels, and permissions there's no way to scope delivery or search, and a flat table can't model who belongs to which channel.
traps.instead: Model workspaces/channels/membership first, then fan a posted message out to that channel's members in real time.

steps.title: 2. Workspaces, Channels, and Permissions
description: The organizing model is workspaces (tenants) containing channels (public or private), with users as members. A membership/permission layer governs who can join, read, post in, and search each channel — this is the multi-tenant backbone Slack/Discord add over plain chat.

Every message operation is permission-checked: can this user post here, read this channel? Membership defines the routing target set (who gets a channel's messages) and the search scope (what a user can find).
Get this first: fanout, history, and search all depend on 'who's allowed in this channel'.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why model permissions/membership before designing message delivery?

concepts.definition: Tenancy + membership + roles that gate read/post/search per channel.
concepts.whyItMatters: Membership defines both delivery targets and search scope — it's foundational, not an add-on.
concepts.example: Private channel: only members route/receive/search its messages.

whyNow: The workspace/channel/permission model is what distinguishes team chat from 1:1; everything else depends on membership.

recap.after: Workspaces/channels with membership + permissions as the backbone.
recap.newRisk: Posting to a channel must reach all members in real time — channel fanout.

steps.title: 3. Channel Fanout over Live Connections
description: Like any chat, clients hold persistent WebSocket connections to gateways. The difference from 1:1: a channel message must reach every member of the channel, not one recipient.

On connect, clients subscribe to their channels in a subscription registry (which gateways hold members of each channel). When a message is posted, the channel service persists it, then the fanout service looks up subscribed members and pushes to their gateways — channel-centric fanout.
For large channels, fanout is the scaling pressure: a message to a 5,000-member channel is 5,000 pushes. Buffer fanout work and push only to online members (offline members read on reconnect).
steps.decisionPrompt: A message is posted to a 5,000-member channel. How does it reach everyone who's online?

concepts.definition: Deliver each channel message to all subscribed members' gateways via a registry of who's subscribed where.
concepts.whyItMatters: Generalizes 1:1 routing to N recipients; the registry makes cross-gateway delivery possible.
concepts.example: Post -> registry: members on gw3, gw7, gw9 -> push to each.

whyNow: Channel fanout is the core real-time difference from WhatsApp; the subscription registry is how it routes across a sharded fleet.

recap.after: Real-time channel fanout to online members.
recap.newRisk: Messages must be durable, ordered, and readable as history — not just pushed live.

flows.name: Post -> persist -> channel fanout to online members
flows.note: A posted message is permission-checked and persisted, then fanned out via the subscription registry to every gateway holding an online member of the channel.

steps.title: 4. Ordered Durable History
description: Unlike ephemeral chat, channel history is first-class: users scroll back, see context, and search. Persist every message durably before acking, assigning a per-channel sequence so history is consistently ordered for everyone.

Shard messages by channel so a channel's history lives together and reads (scrollback, catch-up on reconnect) are efficient. Dedup on a client message id so retries don't duplicate, and track each user's unread cursor (last-read message id) for unread badges.
Offline/returning members read missed messages from the store by sequence — the same persist-then-fanout model where the store is the source of truth and live push is the fast path.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do all members see channel messages in the same order, and how does someone catch up after being offline?

concepts.definition: Monotonic per-channel sequence + per-user last-read cursor.
concepts.whyItMatters: Consistent ordering across members and accurate unread/catch-up state.

whyNow: Durable ordered history is what makes channels searchable and catch-up-able; it's the data backbone search builds on.

recap.newRisk: Searching that history must respect permissions and stay fast.

steps.title: 5. Permission-Scoped Search
description: Search is first-class in team chat. As messages are posted, an indexer writes them to a full-text search index. But results must respect access: a user must never find messages from channels they can't access.

Scope at query time: resolve the requester's accessible channels (from membership) and filter the search to those channels — or partition the index by channel and query only permitted partitions. Never return a hit the user couldn't open.
Index asynchronously (the message store is the truth); search can lag posting by seconds. This is the inverted-index + permission-filter pattern applied to chat.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you let users search history without ever surfacing a message from a private channel they're not in?

concepts.definition: Full-text index filtered to the requester's accessible channels at query time.
concepts.whyItMatters: Search must respect ACLs — a leak here is a serious privacy bug.
concepts.example: Query 'deploy' AND channel IN (user's accessible channels).

whyNow: Searchable history is a defining team-chat feature, and permission-scoping is the correctness twist that 1:1 chat doesn't have.

recap.newRisk: Presence/typing is high-frequency and shouldn't ride the durable path.

flows.name: Async index, then permission-scoped query
flows.note: Messages are indexed asynchronously off the post path; at query time results are filtered to the channels the requester can access.

steps.title: 6. Presence and Typing
description: Awareness — who's online/away, who's typing — is high-frequency and ephemeral. It does not go through the durable message path; presence updates are best-effort and lossy (missing one momentarily is harmless).

Derive presence from connection liveness (heartbeats on the live socket) and broadcast changes to relevant channels/workspaces, throttled. Typing indicators are sent to the channel's online members and expire quickly.
Scope and throttle broadcasts: don't fan out every typing event to a huge channel — cap presence fanout so awareness doesn't dominate traffic.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should 'user is typing' go through the same durable, persisted path as messages?
whyNow: Presence is a core feature with a distinct (ephemeral, best-effort) consistency profile; separating it keeps the durable path clean and cheap.

concepts.definition: Best-effort, non-durable online/typing state derived from connection liveness.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps awareness cheap and off the durable message path.
concepts.example: Typing event throttled, broadcast to online channel members, auto-expires.

recap.newRisk: Large channels and gateway failures stress fanout and reconnection at scale.

steps.title: 7. Scaling Large Channels and Gateways
description: Large channels make fanout the bottleneck: one message to thousands of members is thousands of pushes, amplified by busy channels. Buffer fanout via a queue with scalable workers; push only to online members and let others catch up from history on reconnect.

Shard messages by channel and the gateway fleet by connection; the subscription registry is rebuildable from gateways re-registering. Very large 'announcement' channels may switch to a pull/read model (members fetch rather than receive a push) when fanout cost is too high.

Gateways are thin and replaceable: on failure, clients reconnect to another gateway, re-subscribe, and catch up from the durable store — a brief reconnect, not lost messages. Presence fanout is throttled to avoid amplification.
steps.decisionPrompt: An @everyone post hits a 50,000-member channel. What's the cost, and how do you keep it from melting the system?
whyNow: Closing on large-channel fanout + gateway failover shows the design scales where team chat actually hurts (big channels) and degrades gracefully.

recap.after: Buffered fanout, rebuildable routing, reconnect-tolerant, pull for huge channels.
recap.newRisk: Fanout lag on huge channels and reconnect storms — bounded by queuing, pull-mode, and catch-up-from-history.

bottlenecks.issue: Fanout to a very large channel.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Queue + scalable fanout workers; pull/read model for huge channels.
Thin gateways + rebuildable subscription registry + client reconnect with catch-up.

failureDrills.scenario: A gateway with many connections crashes.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Clients reconnect to other gateways, re-subscribe, and catch up missed messages from the store.
failureDrills.mitigation: Durable history + rebuildable registry + reconnect with last-read cursor.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Team Chat
finalDesign.description: Workspaces contain channels with membership/permissions that gate posting, delivery, and search. Clients hold WebSocket connections to thin gateways and subscribe to their channels in a subscription registry. A posted message is permission-checked, persisted durably with a per-channel sequence (dedup by client id), then the fanout service pushes it to all online subscribed members (offline members catch up from history via their last-read cursor). An async indexer powers permission-scoped full-text search. Presence/typing is ephemeral and best-effort, off the durable path. Large channels buffer fanout (or switch to pull), gateways are replaceable, and the subscription registry is rebuildable — so the system scales and reconnects without losing messages.

say: Confirm channel-centric (not 1:1) with workspaces, permissions, and searchable history.
Pin real-time fanout and permission-correctness as the goals.
Note large channels are the scaling challenge.
Tradeoffs: push vs pull for huge channels, search index freshness, presence fanout cost.
Mention threads, reactions, and E2E encryption as extensions.

expectations: Uses WebSockets and fans out channel messages.
May miss subscription routing, permission-scoped search, large-channel scaling.
Reasons about large-channel fanout (push vs pull).
Discusses permission-scoped search correctness and presence amplification precisely.

followUps: How do you handle fanout for a 50K-member announcement channel?
How do you keep search results consistent with channel permission changes?
How would you add threads and reactions to the model?
How do you keep presence accurate without flooding large channels?
How would you support cross-workspace shared channels?
